title: EXPOSÉ XI COMPARISON WITH CLASSICAL COHOMOLOGY: CASE OF A SMOOTH SCHEME

pages.title: EXPOSÉ XI COMPARISON WITH CLASSICAL COHOMOLOGY: CASE OF A SMOOTH SCHEME

blocks.html:
EXPOSÉ XI
COMPARISON WITH CLASSICAL COHOMOLOGY: CASE OF A SMOOTH SCHEMEby M. Artin
Contents

1. Introduction
2. Existence of sufficiently general hyperplane sections
3. Construction of good neighborhoods
4. The comparison theorem
1. Introduction

In no 4 we shall prove that étale cohomology with values in a locally constant torsion sheaf coincides with classical cohomology for a scheme \(X\) smooth over \(\operatorname{Spec} \mathbb{C}\). The proof, which is essentially the one given in [1], is elementary starting from Grauert's theorem recalled below (4.3 (iii)), which says that every finite covering of \(X(\mathbb{C})\) comes from an étale covering of \(X\) ; this is why we give it here, although we shall prove a more complete result later, using Hironaka's resolution of singularities theorem [3] (*). Moreover, the good neighborhoods constructed in section 3 could be useful in other situations.

Let us note that it is not true that étale cohomology and classical cohomology coincide for sheaves which are not torsion. For example, one has \(H^1(X_{\text{ét}}, \mathbb{Z}) = 0\) for every normal scheme \(X\) (IX 3.6 (ii)), but classical cohomology is not necessarily zero.

footnotes.html: (*) Let us note moreover that Hironaka's resolution of singularities also makes it possible to give a very simple proof of Grauert's result, and in this respect it may be regarded as being in any case at the basis of the comparison theorems. See on this subject the exposé of Mme M. Raynaud in SGA 1 XII (North Holland Pub. Cie).

pages.title: 2. Existence of sufficiently general hyperplane sections

blocks.html: 2. Existence of sufficiently general hyperplane sections
The following result is classical but a reference is lacking (while awaiting EGA V):

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 2.1

Let \(k\) be a field and \(V \subset \mathbb{P}_k^n\) a projective algebraic scheme purely of dimension \(r\). Let \(V'\) be the open subscheme of points of \(V\) smooth over \(\operatorname{Spec} k\) and let \(P\) be a rational point of \(\mathbb{P}^n\). Then:

  1. (i) A "sufficiently general" linear subspace \(L\) of given dimension \(d\) of \(\mathbb{P}^n\) meets \(V\) transversally at each point of \(L \cap V'\).
  2. (ii) Let \(\{H_1,\dots,H_s\}\) \((s \leq r)\) be a system of hyperplanes of degree \(d \geq 2\) passing through \(P\) and let \(Y = H_1 \cap \dots \cap H_s\). If \(\{H_1,\dots,H_s\}\) is sufficiently general, then \(Y\) meets \(V\) transversally at each point of \(Y \cap V'\).

The locution "for a sufficiently general linear subspace" means that there exists a dense open subset \(U\) of the Grassmannian which parametrizes the linear subspaces of dimension \(d\), such that the assertion under consideration about \(L\) is true whenever the parameter of \(L\) is in \(U\). This therefore does not imply that such an \(L\) exists (defined over \(k\)), if \(k\) is not infinite. Similarly, the hyperplanes of degree \(d\) passing through \(P\) are parametrized by a projective space \(\mathbb{P}^N\), and the locution "for \(\{H_1,\dots,H_s\}\) sufficiently general" means that there exists a dense open subset \(U\) of \((\mathbb{P}^N)^s\) such that the assertion under consideration is true whenever the parameter of \((H_1,\dots,H_s)\) is in \(U\).

Proof (i): By induction one is immediately reduced to the case where \(L\) is a hyperplane. Let \((V'_ u)\) be a finite open covering of \(V'\). If assertion (i) is true when one replaces the symbol \(V'\) by \(V'_ u\) for every \( u\), it is also true for \(V'\), because the intersection of a finite number of dense open subsets is likewise open and dense. One may therefore replace the situation by an affine algebraic scheme, say \(V \subset \mathbb{A}_k^n = \operatorname{Spec} k[x_1,\dots,x_n]\). The hyperplane \(L\) will be the set of zeros of a linear equation

Recall that a smooth scheme is locally a complete intersection. One may therefore replace \(V'\) by an open subset of points of \(V\) such that there exist polynomials \(f_1(x),\dots,f_{n-r}(x)\) which vanish on \(V\) and indices \(1 \leq j_\nu \leq n\) \((\nu = 1,\dots,n-r)\) such that the determinant
\[\left| \frac{\partial f_i}{\partial x_{j_\nu}} \right| \qquad (i,\nu = 1,\dots,n-r)\]
is invertible. Say \(j_ u = u\) \(( u = 1,\dots,n-r)\). Then one can solve the equations
\[y_j = \sum_{i=1}^{n-r} c_i\, \frac{\partial f_i}{\partial x_j}\]
for \(c_i\) as a rational function of the variables \(x_i\), \(y_j\) \((i = 1,\dots,n-r\) ; \(j = 1,\dots,n)\), and these functions \(c_i(x,y)\) are defined at each point of \(V'\) .
Now to say that \(L\) meets \(V\) transversally at a point \(Q\) of \(V'\) amounts to saying that the value of the matrix

\[\begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{\partial f_1}{\partial x_1} & \cdots & \dfrac{\partial f_1}{\partial x_n} \\[1.2em] \vdots & & \vdots \\[0.8em] \dfrac{\partial f_{n-r}}{\partial x_1} & & \dfrac{\partial f_{n-r}}{\partial x_n} \\[1.2em] a_1 & \cdots & a_n \end{bmatrix}\]
at \(Q\) is of rank \(n-r+1\). Thus for \(L\) to meet \(V\) transversally at each point of \(V'\) it is necessary and sufficient that the system of \(r+1\) equations
\[a_j = \sum_{i=1}^{n-r} c_i(x,a)\, \frac{\partial f_i}{\partial x_j} \qquad (j = n-r+1,\dots,n)\] \[a_0 + \sum_{i=1}^n a_i x_i = 0\]

has no solution on \(V'\). Such a condition is obviously constructible in \((a)\): Let \(X = V' \times A\), where \(A\) is the affine space of coordinates \(a_0,\dots,a_n\), and let \(Y \subset X\) be the closed subset of solutions of these equations. The condition is that the fiber of \(Y/A\) is empty, which is indeed a constructible condition (EGA IV 9.5.1). It therefore suffices to check that the fiber above the generic point of \(A\) is empty, which is seen immediately from the form of the equations. This completes the proof of (i).

(ii): Consider the rational map \(\mathbb{P}^n \dashrightarrow \mathbb{P}^N\) given by the linear system of hyperplanes of degree \(d\) \((d \geq 2)\) passing through \(P\). It is well known (and one checks it easily) that it is a "blowing-up" of \(P\), hence gives a locally closed immersion
\[f : \mathbb{P}^n - \{P\} \longrightarrow \mathbb{P}^N .\]

Let \(V'' = V' - (\{P\} \cap V')\), \(\overline{V}'' = f(V'')\) and let \(L_i\) be the hyperplane of \(\mathbb{P}^N\) corresponding to the hyperplanes \(H_i\) \((i = 1,\dots,s)\). Then \(V''\) is isomorphic to \(\overline{V}''\), and by the definition of \(f\), to say that \(Y\) meets \(V''\) transversally amounts to saying that \(L_1 \cap \dots \cap L_s\) meets \(\overline{V}''\) transversally. Setting \(\overline{V} = \) the closure of \(\overline{V}''\), one is reduced to assertion (i) for \(\overline{V}\), which implies (ii) with \(V''\) in place of \(V'\). It therefore remains to consider the point \(P\), if it is a smooth point of \(V\). But it is obvious that if \(\{H_1,\dots,H_s\}\) is sufficiently general and \(P\) is smooth on \(V\), then \(Y\) meets \(V\) transversally at \(P\), whence the result.
3. Construction of good neighborhoods

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 3.1

One calls an elementary fibration a morphism of schemes \(f : X \longrightarrow S\) which can be embedded in a commutative diagram

\[\xymatrix{ X \ar[r]^-{j} \ar[dr]_-{f} & \overline{X} \ar[d]^-{\bar{f}} & Y \ar[l]_-{i} \ar[dl]^-{g} \\ & S & }\]

satisfying the following conditions:

  1. (i) \(j\) is an open immersion dense in each fiber, and \(X = \overline{X} - Y\).
  2. (ii) \(\bar{f}\) is smooth and projective, with irreducible geometric fibers of dimension 1 .
  3. (iii) \(g\) is an étale covering, and each fiber of \(g\) is non-empty.
One checks easily that such an embedding of \(X\) is unique if it exists, up to canonical isomorphism.

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 3.2

One calls a good neighborhood relative to \(S\) an \(S\)-scheme \(X\) such that there exist \(S\)-schemes

\[X = X_n,\dots,X_0 = S\]

and elementary fibrations \(f_i : X_i \longrightarrow X_{i-1}\) , \(i = 1,\dots,n\).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 3.3 (*)

Let \(k\) be an algebraically closed field, \(X/\operatorname{Spec} k\) a smooth scheme, and \(x \in X\) a rational point. There exists an open subset of \(X\) containing \(x\) which is a good neighborhood (relative to \(\operatorname{Spec} k\)).

Proof. Take \(X\) irreducible. By induction on \(\dim X = n\) it suffices to find a neighborhood \(U\) of \(x\) in \(X\) and an elementary fibration \(f : U \longrightarrow V\), with \(V\) smooth and of dimension \(n-1\). Indeed, there will exist a neighborhood \(V'\) of \(v = f(x)\) which is a good neighborhood, and one may take \(U' = U \cap V'\) as a good neighborhood of \(x\).

One may suppose \(X \subset \mathbb{A}^r\) affine. Let \(X_0\) be the closure of \(X\) in \(\mathbb{P}^r\). Let \(\overline{X}\) be the normalization of \(X_0\) and \(Y = \overline{X} - X\), with the reduced induced structure. Let moreover \(S \subset \overline{X}\) be the closed subset of singular points. One has \(S \subset Y\) and
\[\begin{gathered} \dim \overline{X} = \dim X = n,\\ \dim Y = n-1,\\ \dim S \leq n-2. \end{gathered}\]

Let us embed \(\overline{X}\) in a projective space \(\mathbb{P}^N\), by means of an invertible sheaf \(L\) which is of the form \(M^{\otimes r}\) with \(M\) very ample and \(r \geq 2\). There then exist hyperplanes \(H_1,\dots,H_{n-1}\) of \(\mathbb{P}^N\), where \(H_i\) is the set of zeros of

which contain \(x\) and such that the intersection \(L = H_1 \cap \dots \cap H_{n-1}\) is of dimension \(N-n+1\) and meets \(\overline{X}\) and \(Y\) transversally (2.1). The intersection \(\overline{X} \cap L\) is a smooth connected curve (this follows from Bertini's theorem), and \(Y \cap L\) is of dimension 0. Let us moreover choose another hyperplane
such that \(H_0\) meets \(X \cap L\) transversally and \(H_0 \cap Y \cap L = \emptyset\) .
Consider the projection \(\mathbb{P}^N \longrightarrow \mathbb{P}^{n-1}\) obtained by "introducing the projective coordinates"

This is a rational map defined outside the center of projection \(C = H_0 \cap \dots \cap H_{n-1}\) . Let \(\varepsilon : P' \longrightarrow \mathbb{P}^N\) be the blowing-up of \(C\), such that one has a diagram
\[\xymatrix{ \mathbb{P}^N \ar@{-->}[dr] & & P' \ar[ll]_-{\varepsilon} \ar[dl]^-{\pi} \\ & \mathbb{P}^{n-1} & }\]

where \(\pi\) is a morphism. Let \(\overline{X}' \subset P'\) be the "proper" inverse image of \(\overline{X}\), that is, the closure of \(\varepsilon^{-1}(\overline{X} - (\overline{X} \cap C))\). Since by hypothesis \(C\) meets \(\overline{X}\) transversally, the morphism \(\overline{X}' \longrightarrow \overline{X}\) is a blowing-up of the finite set \(\overline{X} \cap C\). Let \(X' = X - \overline{X} \cap C\), which is also identified with an open subscheme of \(\overline{X}'\), and \(Y' = \overline{X}' - X'\) the closed subscheme with the reduced induced structure. One has a diagram \(\mathbb{D}\) of morphisms
\[\xymatrix{ X' \ar[r]^-{j} \ar[dr]_-{f'} & \overline{X}' \ar[d]^-{\bar{f}'} & Y' \ar[l]_-{i} \ar[dl]^-{g'} \\ & \mathbb{P}^{n-1} & }\]

and I claim that there exists a neighborhood \(V\) of \(v = f'(x)\) such that the restriction of \(\mathbb{D}\) to \(V\) satisfies conditions (i), (ii), (iii) of (3.1), hence that \(f'|V\) is an elementary fibration. This will complete the proof.

Condition (i) will be trivial. For (ii), let us note that \(\overline{X} \cap L\) is a smooth curve by hypothesis, and one sees immediately that one has a one-to-one morphism \(\bar{f}'^{-1}(v) \longrightarrow \overline{X} \cap L\) induced by \(\varepsilon\) . Thus \((\bar{f}'^{-1}(v))_{\mathrm{red}} \to \overline{X} \cap L\) is bijective. To check that \(\bar{f}'\) is smooth above a neighborhood of \(v\), it suffices by Hironaka's lemma (SGA 1 II 2.6 (*)) to check that it is smooth at the generic point of \(\bar{f}'^{-1}(v)\). At this point, \(\overline{X}'\) is isomorphic to \(\overline{X}\), and the morphism is smooth because \(L\) meets \(\overline{X}\) transversally.

It remains to prove that \(g'\) is étale in a neighborhood of \(v\) (since \(Y\) is of dimension \(n-1\), it is obvious that each fiber of \(g'\) is non-empty). One has \(Y' = \varepsilon^{-1}(Y) \amalg D_1 \amalg \dots \amalg D_r\), where \(\overline{X} \cap C = \{P_1,\dots,P_r\}\) and \(D_i\) is the blowing-up of \(P_i\) in \(\overline{X}\). Each \(D_i\) is identified with \(\varepsilon^{-1}(P_i)\), and maps isomorphically onto \(\mathbb{P}^{n-1}\). \(g'\) is defined at each point of \(Y\), and the morphism induced on \(Y\) is none other than \(g'|\varepsilon^{-1}(Y)\). It is étale above \(v\), because \(L\) meets \(Y\) transversally, hence \(g'\) is étale above \(v\).

footnotes.html: (*) This result extends without difficulty to a semi-local assertion.

blocks.html: 4. The comparison theorem

4.0. Let \(X\) be a scheme locally of finite type over \(\operatorname{Spec} \mathbb{C}\) and consider the usual topology on the space \(X(\mathbb{C})\) of rational points of \(X\). We shall denote by \(X_{\text{cl}}\) the site of local isomorphisms \(f : U \longrightarrow X(\mathbb{C})\), that is, of morphisms \(f\) of topological spaces having the following property:
For each \(x \in U\) there exists an open neighborhood \(U_x\) such that \(f|U_x\) is a homeomorphism of \(U_x\) onto an open neighborhood of \(f(x)\).
A family \(\{U_\nu \longrightarrow U\}\) of morphisms of \(X_{\text{cl}}\) is said to be covering if \(U\) is the union of the images of the \(U_\nu\).
Since an open immersion is a local isomorphism, one has a morphism (inclusion) of sites (IV )(*)
\[(4.1) \qquad \delta : X_{\text{cl}} \longrightarrow X(\mathbb{C}) .\]

Now for each local isomorphism \(U \longrightarrow X(\mathbb{C})\) there exists by definition a "covering" of \(U\) by open subsets of \(X(\mathbb{C})\), and one deduces from ( ) that the topoi associated with the two sites are equivalent (by \(\delta_*\)). In particular one may replace \(X(\mathbb{C})\) by \(X_{\text{cl}}\) for the computation of the usual cohomology.

Let now \(f : X' \longrightarrow X\) be an étale morphism of schemes. Then \(f(\mathbb{C}) : X'(\mathbb{C}) \longrightarrow X(\mathbb{C})\) is a local isomorphism. This follows immediately from the Jacobian criterion (SGA 1 II 4.10) and the implicit function theorem. The functor \(X' \mapsto X'(\mathbb{C})\) therefore gives a morphism of sites
\[(4.2) \qquad \varepsilon : X_{\text{cl}} \longrightarrow X_{\text{ét}} ,\]
where \(X_{\text{ét}}\) is the étale site.
Let us recall the following results (**) :

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 4.3
  1. (i) \(X\) is connected and non-empty if and only if \(X(\mathbb{C})\) is connected and non-empty.
  2. (ii) The functor \(\varepsilon\) is fully faithful.
  3. (iii) ("Grauert-Remmert theorem"). The functor \(\varepsilon\) induces an equivalence of the category of finite coverings of \(X(\mathbb{C})\), equipped with its usual topology of a locally compact space, with the category of étale coverings of \(X\).

We shall accept (i) as known. It follows for example from (GAGA) [4]. Assertion (ii) follows easily from (i). Indeed, to check the surjectivity assertion contained in (ii), let \(\varphi : X'(\mathbb{C}) \longrightarrow X''(\mathbb{C})\) be an \(X(\mathbb{C})\)-morphism. One may suppose all the schemes connected and affine, hence separated. Then the graph \(\Gamma\) of \(\varphi\) is a connected component of \(X'(\mathbb{C}) \times_{X(\mathbb{C})} X''(\mathbb{C})\), whence \(\Gamma = Y(\mathbb{C})\) for a certain connected component \(Y\) of \(X' \times_X X''\), by (i). Then \(Y\) is the graph of a morphism \(f : X' \longrightarrow X''\) which induces \(\varphi\), i.e. the projection \(Y \longrightarrow X'\) is an isomorphism: indeed \(Y(\mathbb{C}) \longrightarrow X'(\mathbb{C})\) is an isomorphism, from which one easily deduces that the same holds for \(Y \longrightarrow X'\) (which is étale, radicial, surjective!). For (iii) everything amounts, by (ii), to proving that each finite covering of \(X(\mathbb{C})\) is of the form \(X'(\mathbb{C})\) where \(X' \longrightarrow X\) is étale.

For this, one reduces immediately by descent (SGA 1 IX 4.7) to the case where \(X\) is connected and normal. The problem is local on \(X\) (for the Zariski topology), and one may therefore suppose \(X\) affine. Let \(f : X \longrightarrow \mathbb{A}^n\) be a finite morphism, and \(T' \longrightarrow X(\mathbb{C})\) a connected finite covering. One observes immediately that the composite morphism \(\eta' : T' \longrightarrow \mathbb{A}^n(\mathbb{C})\) is an "analytic covering" in the sense of ([2] § 2, Def. 3). By ([2] § 2, Satz 8) \(\eta'\) extends to an "analytic covering" \(\eta : T \longrightarrow \mathbb{P}^n(\mathbb{C})\), and it suffices to prove that \(\eta\) is induced by a suitable morphism of schemes \(f : Y \longrightarrow \mathbb{P}^n\), with \(Y\) normal. Now the fundamental theorem ([2] § 13 Satz 42) asserts that there is a canonical normal analytic structure on \(T\) such that \(\eta\) induces a morphism of analytic spaces. Since then \(\eta\) is a finite morphism, the direct image of the structure sheaf on \(T\) is a sheaf \(\mathcal{A}\) of coherent analytic algebras on \(\mathbb{P}^n\). It follows from GAGA [4] that \(\mathcal{A}\) is the analytic sheaf associated with an algebraic sheaf \(A\) on \(\mathbb{P}^n\), and one takes \(Y = \operatorname{Spec} A\).
We can now prove the following result:

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 4.4

Let \(X\) be a smooth scheme over \(\operatorname{Spec} \mathbb{C}\).

  1. (i) There is an equivalence between the category of constructible locally constant torsion sheaves on \(X_{\text{ét}}\) and the category of locally constant torsion sheaves, with finite fibers, on \(X_{\text{cl}}\), the equivalence being given by the quasi-inverse functors \({}_*\) and \({}^*\) .
  2. (ii) Let \(F\) be a locally constant torsion sheaf, with finite fibers, on \(X_{\text{cl}}\), and let us also denote by \(F\) the sheaf \(\varepsilon_* F\) induced on \(X_{\text{ét}}\). Then \[R^q \varepsilon_* F = 0 \qquad \text{if } q > 0 .\]
  3. (iii) With the notations of (i), the canonical morphism \[H^q(X_{\text{ét}}, F) \longrightarrow H^q(X_{\text{cl}}, F)\] is bijective for each \(q\). In particular, \[H^q(X_{\text{ét}}, \mathbb{Z}/n) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } H^q(X_{\text{cl}}, \mathbb{Z}/n)\] for each \(q\).

Proof. For (i), let us note that the sheaves in question are those which are representable by étale coverings of \(X\) (resp. by finite coverings of \(X(\mathbb{C})\)). Since there is an equivalence between the categories of these coverings (4.3), assertion (i) follows immediately. The isomorphism of (iii) is a consequence of (ii) and of the Leray spectral sequence V 5.2 for \(\varepsilon\). It therefore remains to prove (ii).

Now \(R^q \varepsilon_* F\) is the sheaf associated with the presheaf \(\mathcal{R}^q F\), where \((\mathcal{R}^q F)(X') = H^q(X'_{\text{cl}}, F)\) for \(X' \longrightarrow X\) étale. To prove that \(R^q \varepsilon_* F = 0\) if \(q > 0\), one must therefore prove the following:

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 4.5

Let \(\xi \in H^q(X_{\text{cl}}, F)\) and \(x \in X_{\text{cl}} = X(\mathbb{C})\). There exists an étale morphism \(X' \longrightarrow X\) whose image contains \(x\) and such that the image of \(\xi\) in \(H^q(X'_{\text{cl}}, F)\) is zero.

Proof of the lemma. Induction on \(n = \dim X\) ; the problem is local on \(X\) for the étale topology, and one may therefore suppose \(F\) constant, and by dévissage one may even suppose that \(F = \mathbb{Z}/n\). Moreover, replacing \(X\) by a Zariski open neighborhood of \(x\), one may (3.3) suppose that \(X\) admits an elementary fibration (3.1) \(f : X \longrightarrow S\). Let us resume the notations of (3.1):

by direct computation, one sees that \(R^q j_{\text{cl}*} F = 0\) if \(q > 1\), that \(R^1 j_{\text{cl}*} F\) is the extension by 0 of a constant sheaf on \(Y_{\text{cl}}\), and finally that \(j_{\text{cl}*} F\) is the constant sheaf \(\mathbb{Z}/n\) on \(\overline{X}\) . Moreover, one can compute \(R^q \overline{f}_{\text{cl}*}(j_{\text{cl}*} F)\) fiber by fiber. From the Leray spectral sequence
\[E_2^{pq} = R^p \overline{f}_{\text{cl}*}(R^q j_{\text{cl}*} F) \implies R^{p+q} f_{\text{cl}*} F\]
one deduces that one can likewise compute \(R^{p+q} f_{\text{cl}*} F\) fiber by fiber, and hence that

\[R^0 f_{\text{cl}*} \mathbb{Z}/n = \mathbb{Z}/n ,\] \[R^1 f_{\text{cl}*} \mathbb{Z}/n \quad \text{est un faisceau localement constant de torsion sur } S_{\text{cl}} ,\] \[R^q f_{\text{cl}*} \mathbb{Z}/n = 0 \quad \text{si } q > 1 .\]
The Leray spectral sequence
\[E_2^{pq} = H^p(S_{\text{cl}}, R^q f_{\text{cl}*} F) \implies H^{p+q}(X_{\text{cl}}, F)\]
thus reduces to the exact sequence
\[(*) \quad \dots \longrightarrow H^q(S_{\text{cl}}, f_{\text{cl}*} F) \longrightarrow H^q(X_{\text{cl}}, F) \longrightarrow H^{q-1}(S_{\text{cl}}, R^1 f_{\text{cl}*} F) \longrightarrow\]

Let \(s\) be the image of \(x\) in \(S\). By the induction hypothesis and thanks to (i), there exists for each class \(\eta \in H^q(S_{\text{cl}}, G)\) (\(G\) locally constant torsion, with finite fibers) an "étale neighborhood" \(S' \longrightarrow S\) of \(s\) such that the image of \(\eta\) in \(H^q(S'_{\text{cl}}, G)\) is zero. Now the functors \(R^q f_{\text{cl}*}\) obviously commute with étale base changes, and if \(S' \longrightarrow S\) is an étale neighborhood of \(s\), then \(X' = X \times_S S' \longrightarrow X\) is an étale neighborhood of \(x\). When \(q \geq 1\) one therefore concludes thanks to the exact sequence (*) by taking first \(G = R^1 f_{\text{cl}*} F\) and then \(G = f_{\text{cl}*} F\) . When \(q = 1\), one notes that \(H^1(X_{\text{cl}}, \mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z})\) (resp. \(H^1(X, \mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z})\)) classifies the principal étale coverings of \(X_{\text{cl}}\) (resp. \(X\)), with group \(\mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z}\), hence by virtue of 4.3 (iii) the map \(H^1(X, \mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z}) \longrightarrow H^1(X_{\text{cl}}, \mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z})\) is bijective, whence immediately the effacement result 4.5 for \(q = 1\).

blocks.title: Variant
blocks.html:
Variant 4.6

One can also prove (4.5) in the following elegant manner, due to Serre: one remarks that a connected good neighborhood (3.2) \(X\) satisfies the two following conditions:

  1. (i) \(\pi_n(X(\mathbb{C})) = 0\) if \(n > 1\),
  2. (ii) \(\pi_1(X(\mathbb{C}))\) is a successive extension of free groups of finite type.

Indeed, if \(X \longrightarrow S\) is an elementary fibration where \(S\) is a good neighborhood, one may suppose (i) and (ii) true for \(S\) by induction. Now \(X(\mathbb{C}) \longrightarrow S(\mathbb{C})\) is a locally trivial fiber space, as one sees easily, with fibers isomorphic to \(X_0(\mathbb{C})\), where \(X_0\) is an arbitrary fiber of \(X/S\). Now \(X_0\) is a connected non-complete non-singular curve and it is well known that this implies that \(\pi_n(X_0(\mathbb{C})) = 0\) if \(n > 1\), and that \(\pi_1(X_0(\mathbb{C}))\) is a free group. Then (i) and (ii) are consequences of the homotopy exact sequence
\[\longrightarrow \pi_n(X_0(\mathbb{C})) \longrightarrow \pi_n(X(\mathbb{C})) \longrightarrow \pi_n(S(\mathbb{C})) \longrightarrow \dots\]

Thus \(X(\mathbb{C})\) is a \(K(\pi,1)\) space, and this implies that each cohomology class \(\xi \in H^n(X(\mathbb{C}), F)\) becomes zero on the universal covering of \(X(\mathbb{C})\). It suffices to prove that \(\xi\) already becomes zero on a finite covering \(X'\) of \(X\), which amounts to the same as proving that \(\pi_1 = \pi_1(X(\mathbb{C}))\) is a good group (cf. CG I 16), that is, that the morphism from \(\pi_1\) into its completion \(\hat{\pi}_1 = \varprojlim_N \pi_1/N\) (where \(N\) runs through the set of invariant subgroups of \(\pi_1\) of finite index) induces a bijection
\[H^q(\hat{\pi}_1, M) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } H^q(\pi_1, M)\]

for every continuous finite \(\pi_1\)-module \(M\) (where the left-hand symbol is the cohomology of \(\hat{\pi}_1\) as a profinite group). Now a successive extension of free groups of finite type is a good group (cf. CG I p. 15-16, exc. 1,2), whence the result.

footnotes.html: (*) More precisely, the inclusion of categories \(\operatorname{Ouv}(X(\mathbb{C})) \longrightarrow X_{\text{cl}}\) defines a morphism of sites (4.1) in the reverse direction.
(**) Which appear in the exposé cited in the footnote, p. 2.

blocks.html: Bibliography
[1] GIRAUD J., Analysis Situs, Séminaire Bourbaki, Exposé no 256.
[2] GRAUERT H et REMMERT R., Komplexe Räume, Math. Ann. Bd. 136, p. 345 (1958).
[3] HIRONAKA H., Resolution of singularities of an algebraic variety over a field of characteristic zero, Annals of Math. Vol. 39, no 1,2 (1964).
[4] SERRE J.P., Géométrie Algébrique et Géométrie Analytique, Annales de l'Institut Fourier, t. VI, p. 1-12 (1956).
